title: Arabic Numbers and Verb Negation: Demonstrative Questions and Family Terms
summary: This session covers two main topics: using ordinal numbers for dates and answering rhetorical questions. Students learn to express ordinals like 'eighth day' (اليوم الثامن) and how negation with 'ma' works in past tense verbs. The teacher clarifies family terms (khali, ibn) and corrects common mistakes in verb forms.
key_points: Ordinal numbers are formed using specific constructs: e.g., **الثاني** (second), **الثالث** (third). For females: **الثانية** (second), **الثالثة** (third).
Negating past tense verbs uses **ma-**: e.g., **ما ذهب** (he did not go), contrasting with **la-** for present/future.
Answering rhetorical questions like **أli sa ka-thaariq?** requires responding with **barah** to affirm the implied negation.
Family terms include distinguishing **khali** (maternal uncle) and proper verb agreement when describing females, e.g., **الصفحة fourth** becomes **الصفحة الرابعة**.
questions: [00:03:01] Q: Does the change for ordinal numbers only apply to 'al-awwald? — A: Teacher explains that most ordinals use ta marbuta for females, e.g., **ثانية**, not just 'al-awwald'.
[00:07:00] Q: How to answer rhetorical questions like 'Ali saka-thaariq?' — A: Use 'barah' to agree, negating the implied negative.
